questions.question: Why can a JPA @Entity not be a Java record?
choices: JPA requires a no-arg constructor and mutable fields (for dirty checking and lazy loading); records are final and immutable.
JPA only works with classes from the java.util package.
Records can't be persisted to relational databases.
questions.explanation: JPA needs a no-arg constructor and the ability to set fields after construction (for both loading and dirty checking). Records are final and have only the canonical constructor - incompatible with the JPA lifecycle. Use classes for entities, records for DTOs.

questions.question: You load an Order with @OneToMany lineItems and access order.getLineItems() inside the controller's JSON serialization. You see 'LazyInitializationException: could not initialize proxy - no Session'. Best fix?
choices: Mark the entity field as transient.
Move the @Transactional to the controller method so the session lives through serialization.

Fetch the collection eagerly in the query (@EntityGraph or 'join fetch' in JPQL), or project to a DTO that doesn't include the lazy collection. Avoid extending the transaction boundary into the controller.
Disable lazy loading globally with hibernate.enable_lazy_load_no_trans=true.
questions.explanation: Fetch eagerly at query time (via @EntityGraph or 'join fetch') or project to a DTO. Extending the transaction into the controller layer (the Open-Session-In-View anti-pattern) is widespread but considered bad practice - long-running transactions, harder error handling, surprising lazy queries during serialisation.

questions.question: Why doesn't a @Transactional method work correctly when called via 'this.transactionalMethod()' from another method in the same class?
choices: Spring requires public visibility; self-invocation downgrades to package-private.
Spring's @Transactional is implemented via a proxy around the bean; self-invocation goes directly to the underlying object and bypasses the proxy, so the transaction interceptor never runs.
Self-invocation always opens a nested transaction, which Spring rejects by default.
questions.explanation: Spring proxies the bean to add transactional behaviour. External callers go through the proxy; self-calls (this.x()) bypass it, so the transaction interceptor isn't invoked. The fix is to call via an injected reference or move the transactional method to a separate bean.
